prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response: You probably think that the vivid, detailed picture you have of your own future—where you’ll be, what you’ll feel, who will be there—is a reliable guide to what will actually happen. It isn’t. The brain happily stitches together the shakiest scraps of evidence into a widescreen preview, then stamps it “highly probable.” In reality, your future self is being written by forces you can’t yet detect: chance meetings, microscopic bodily changes, a news headline you haven’t read. The clarity of the mental movie is a mirage; the only thing high-resolution about it is your confidence.
